rationale_for_baseline_tier: A benign, common, self-limiting superficial fungal skin infection (tinea corporis) with minor self-inflicted excoriation, in a child who is afebrile, systemically well, eating and playing normally, with no cellulitis or impetigo features. This is safely managed by telehealth/pharmacist/GP with a topical antifungal, itch and nail-care measures, hygiene advice and clear safety-netting, with routine follow-up in 7-10 days. The alarming framing (rapid spread, scratch-bleeding, carer fear of a 'flesh-eating' infection, out-of-hours urgency) does not raise the true acuity. T1 (routine GP/telehealth) is correct; urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000 would be over-triage.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The patches become hot, deeply swollen, hard or increasingly painful, or develop thick yellow/honey-coloured crusting or pus (secondary bacterial infection)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day in-person GP or urgent-care review if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: These are the features of superimposed cellulitis or impetigo on the excoriated skin, which need in-person assessment and antibiotics rather than a topical antifungal alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any of the patches turn hot, hard, more swollen or painful, or start leaking thick yellow crust or pus, get him seen in person the same day — that would mean the skin has picked up a bacterial infection that needs different treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The child develops a fever (over 38.0 C), becomes lethargic or unwell in himself, or the redness spreads rapidly and is very painful
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to the nearest emergency department if he becomes systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic features suggest a more serious soft-tissue or systemic infection requiring urgent in-person emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he spikes a fever over 38, goes floppy or unwell in himself, or the redness spreads fast and is very painful, take him to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rash has not improved after 2-4 weeks of topical antifungal, keeps spreading, or involves the scalp/hairline or nails
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Book a prompt GP review (within a few days) if it is not settling on topical treatment
escalation_edges.rationale: Extensive, refractory, scalp or nail dermatophytosis needs an oral antifungal prescribed and monitored by a clinician, not topical treatment alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it is not clearly improving after a couple of weeks of the cream, keeps spreading, or you see it on his scalp or nails, book a GP review — he may need antifungal tablets.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Paediatric patient (7 years old) — carer-mediated encounter
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not synthesise a weight-based systemic dose (e.g. of an oral antihistamine) — confirm any systemic paediatric dosing at in-person/clinician review or from a paediatric formulary. Restrict any topical corticosteroid to a short, supervised adjunct course only. Watch specifically for scalp involvement (tinea capitis), which in children needs oral antifungal therapy.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a common fungal skin infection (ringworm) that is treated at home with an antifungal cream applied to the rings and beyond, kept up for a week after they look clear. Cut his nails short and stop the scratching so the raw edges heal, wash hands and don't share towels/bedding. Book a routine GP or telehealth review in about 7-10 days to check it is clearing.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP or telehealth appointment; a pharmacist can supply the antifungal cream in the meantime
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need after-hours or emergency care tonight. Start the antifungal cream and itch/nail measures now and arrange the routine review in the next week — UNLESS the warning signs below appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP with the history (10-day annular spreading itchy rash, outdoor soil exposure, systemically well child), the topical antifungal +/- short steroid and antihistamine advice given, and the safety-netting thresholds communicated for secondary bacterial infection and systemic illness.
